revenue.label: Revenue
revenue.title: Revenue (Total Sales)
revenue.definition: The total money a company earns from selling its products or services, before subtracting any costs. It's the 'top line' of the income statement.
revenue.example: If Apple sells 10 million iPhones at $1,000 each, that's $10B in revenue, regardless of how much it cost to make them.

netIncome.label: Net Income
netIncome.title: Net Income (Bottom Line)
netIncome.definition: The actual profit left after subtracting ALL costs: production, salaries, taxes, interest, and everything else. This is what shareholders truly earn.
netIncome.example: A company with $10B revenue and $7B in total costs has $3B net income. That's 30 cents of profit for every dollar of sales.

freeCashFlow.label: Free Cash Flow
freeCashFlow.title: Free Cash Flow (FCF)
freeCashFlow.definition: The real cash a company generates after paying for operations and investments. Unlike net income, it's very hard to manipulate with accounting tricks.
freeCashFlow.example: A company earns $5B profit but spends $2B on new factories. FCF = $3B. That's the cash available for dividends, buybacks, or debt reduction.

totalEquity.label: Total Equity
totalEquity.title: Total Equity (Book Value)
totalEquity.definition: What shareholders actually own: total assets minus total liabilities. Think of it as the company's net worth if it sold everything and paid all debts.
totalEquity.example: A company with $50B in assets and $30B in debts has $20B equity. If it has 1B shares, book value is $20 per share.

totalLiabilities.label: Total Liabilities
totalLiabilities.title: Total Liabilities (Total Debt)
totalLiabilities.definition: Everything the company owes: loans, bonds, accounts payable, lease obligations, and other financial obligations. High liabilities increase risk in downturns.
totalLiabilities.example: A company with $40B in liabilities and $20B in equity has a 2:1 debt-to-equity ratio, meaning it owes twice as much as it owns.

currentRatio.label: Current Ratio
currentRatio.title: Current Ratio (Liquidity)
currentRatio.definition: Short-term assets divided by short-term debts. Shows if the company can pay bills due within 12 months. Above 1.5 is healthy, below 1.0 is a warning.
currentRatio.example: A company with $6B current assets and $4B current liabilities has a current ratio of 1.5, so it can comfortably cover its near-term obligations.

interestCoverage.label: Interest Coverage
interestCoverage.title: Interest Coverage Ratio
interestCoverage.definition: Operating profit divided by interest payments. Shows how easily the company can pay interest on its debt. Above 3x is comfortable, below 1.5x is risky.
interestCoverage.example: If a company earns $9B operating income and pays $3B in interest, coverage = 3x. It earns three times its interest payments.

debtToEbitda.label: Debt/EBITDA
debtToEbitda.title: Debt-to-EBITDA Ratio
debtToEbitda.definition: Total debt divided by earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization. Shows how many years of earnings it would take to pay off all debt. Below 3x is healthy.
debtToEbitda.example: A company with $15B debt and $5B EBITDA has 3x ratio, meaning it would take 3 years of earnings to pay off debt (if nothing else changed).

peRatio.label: P/E Ratio
peRatio.title: Price-to-Earnings Ratio (P/E)
peRatio.definition: Stock price divided by earnings per share. Shows how much investors pay for each dollar of profit. Lower P/E may signal a bargain, but always compare within the same industry.
peRatio.example: A stock at $150 earning $6/share has P/E = 25. You pay $25 for every $1 of earnings. Tech averages ~25-30, utilities ~15-18.

roe.label: ROE
roe.title: Return on Equity (ROE)
roe.definition: Net income divided by shareholder equity. Measures how efficiently the company uses shareholders' money to generate profit. Above 15% is excellent.
roe.example: A company with $3B profit and $20B equity has ROE = 15%. Every $1 shareholders invested generates 15 cents of annual profit.

pbRatio.label: P/B Ratio
pbRatio.title: Price-to-Book Ratio (P/B)
pbRatio.definition: Stock price divided by book value per share. Below 1.0 means you're paying less than the company's net assets. This could indicate it is potentially undervalued, or it could be a sign of deeper problems.
pbRatio.example: A stock at $40 with book value of $50/share has P/B = 0.8. You're buying $50 of assets for $40, but investigate WHY it's cheap.

pfcf.label: P/FCF
pfcf.title: Price-to-Free Cash Flow (P/FCF)
pfcf.definition: Stock price divided by free cash flow per share. Like P/E but uses real cash instead of accounting earnings. More reliable for spotting true value.
pfcf.example: A stock at $100 with $5 FCF/share has P/FCF = 20. Compare this to P/E: if P/FCF is much higher, profits may not be backed by real cash.

grossMargin.label: Gross Margin
grossMargin.title: Gross Profit Margin
grossMargin.definition: Revenue minus cost of goods, divided by revenue. Shows the basic profitability before overhead. High margins (>40%) often signal strong competitive advantages or pricing power.
grossMargin.example: A software company with 80% gross margin keeps $0.80 of every dollar sold. A retailer at 25% keeps only $0.25. Very different business models.

roic.label: ROIC
roic.title: Return on Invested Capital (ROIC)
roic.definition: Operating profit (after taxes) divided by all invested capital (equity + debt). The best measure of how efficiently management uses ALL capital. Above 10% is strong.
roic.example: A company with $4B operating profit on $30B invested capital has ROIC = 13%. It generates $0.13 of return for every $1 invested.

fairValueLabel.label: Fair Value
fairValueLabel.title: Estimated Fair Value
fairValueLabel.definition: An estimate of what the stock is truly worth, calculated by combining multiple methods (DCF, Graham, EPV). It's an educated guess, not a guarantee.
fairValueLabel.example: If fair value is $120 and the stock trades at $95, there's a 26% margin of safety, meaning you're potentially buying at a discount.

marginOfSafety.label: Margin of Safety
marginOfSafety.title: Margin of Safety (MoS)
marginOfSafety.definition: How much cheaper the stock price is compared to fair value, as a percentage. Positive = potential bargain. Benjamin Graham recommended buying only with 25%+ margin.
marginOfSafety.example: Fair value $100, current price $75 → MoS = +25%. You're buying with a 25% cushion if your estimate is slightly wrong.

rsi.label: RSI (14)
rsi.title: Relative Strength Index (RSI)
rsi.definition: A momentum indicator from 0-100 that measures speed and magnitude of recent price changes. Below 30 = potentially oversold (buy signal). Above 70 = potentially overbought (caution).
rsi.example: RSI at 25 means the stock has fallen sharply recently and may be oversold. This is a potential entry point if fundamentals are still strong.

sma50.label: SMA 50
sma50.title: 50-Day Simple Moving Average
sma50.definition: The average closing price over the last 50 trading days. Acts as short-term trend indicator. Price above SMA 50 = short-term uptrend.
sma50.example: If stock is at $150 and SMA 50 is $140, the price is trending upward. If it crosses below, it might signal a short-term reversal.

sma200.label: SMA 200
sma200.title: 200-Day Simple Moving Average
sma200.definition: The average closing price over the last 200 trading days. The gold standard for identifying long-term trends. Price above SMA 200 = long-term uptrend.
sma200.example: When SMA 50 crosses above SMA 200 it's called a 'Golden Cross', a bullish signal. The opposite ('Death Cross') is bearish.

macd.label: MACD
macd.title: Moving Average Convergence Divergence
macd.definition: Shows the relationship between two moving averages. The histogram shows momentum strength. Positive and rising = bullish momentum. Negative and falling = bearish.
macd.example: MACD histogram at +2.5 and growing means upward momentum is accelerating. At -1.0 and shrinking, downward pressure is easing.

volatility.label: Volatility
volatility.title: Price Volatility
volatility.definition: Measures how much the stock price swings up and down. High volatility = bigger price swings (more risk and opportunity). Low volatility = more stable price.
volatility.example: A stock with 40% volatility might swing 40% up or down in a year. A utility stock at 15% volatility is much more stable.

momentum.label: Momentum (12m)
momentum.title: 12-Month Price Momentum (ROC)
momentum.definition: How much the stock price has changed over the last 12 months, as a percentage. Positive = price has gained. Negative = price has declined.
momentum.example: Momentum of +25% means the stock is up 25% from one year ago. Strong momentum often persists, but can also mean the stock is expensive.

dividendYield.label: Dividend Yield
dividendYield.title: Dividend Yield (Annual)
dividendYield.definition: Annual dividend per share divided by the stock price, expressed as a percentage. Shows the cash return you earn just by holding the stock, independent of price changes.
dividendYield.example: A stock at $100 paying $3/year in dividends has a 3% yield. That's $3,000/year on a $100,000 investment, before any price appreciation.

dividendPerShare.label: Dividend Per Share
dividendPerShare.title: Annual Dividend Per Share (DPS)
dividendPerShare.definition: The total dollar amount of dividends paid per share over a year. Rising DPS over time is one of the strongest signals of a healthy, shareholder-friendly company.
dividendPerShare.example: If a company pays $0.25 per quarter, the annual DPS is $1.00. If it was $0.80 last year, dividends grew by 25%.

payoutRatio.label: Payout Ratio
payoutRatio.title: Dividend Payout Ratio
payoutRatio.definition: Percentage of net income paid out as dividends. Below 60% is sustainable, as the company retains enough to reinvest. Above 80% may signal the dividend is at risk of being cut.
payoutRatio.example: A company earning $5/share and paying $2 in dividends has a 40% payout ratio. It keeps $3 to reinvest in growth while rewarding shareholders.

fcfPayout.label: FCF Payout
fcfPayout.title: Free Cash Flow Payout Ratio
fcfPayout.definition: Dividends paid as a percentage of free cash flow. More reliable than the earnings-based payout ratio because FCF represents real cash. Below 70% is healthy.
fcfPayout.example: If FCF is $4/share and dividends are $2/share, FCF payout is 50%. The company generates twice the cash it needs to cover dividends. Very safe.

dividendGrowth.label: Growth (5Y CAGR)
dividendGrowth.title: 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate (CAGR)
dividendGrowth.definition: The compound annual growth rate of dividends over the last 5 years. Shows how fast the company has been increasing its dividend payments to shareholders.
dividendGrowth.example: A 10% CAGR means dividends have doubled roughly every 7 years. Companies with consistent dividend growth often outperform the market long-term.

consecutiveYears.label: Consecutive Years
consecutiveYears.title: Consecutive Years of Dividend Payments
consecutiveYears.definition: How many years in a row the company has paid dividends without interruption. Long streaks (10+ years) signal financial stability and management commitment to shareholders.
consecutiveYears.example: Companies with 25+ consecutive years are called 'Dividend Aristocrats'. They've maintained dividends through recessions, showing exceptional financial resilience.

piotroskiScore.label: Piotroski F-Score
piotroskiScore.title: Piotroski F-Score (Financial Strength)
piotroskiScore.definition: A 0-9 score based on 9 binary criteria covering profitability, leverage, and operating efficiency. Created by Professor Joseph Piotroski to identify financially strong companies. Higher is better.
piotroskiScore.example: A company scoring 8/9 passes almost all financial health tests: positive cash flow, improving margins, low debt, and growing asset turnover. A score of 2/9 fails most, which is a warning sign.

earningsQuality.label: Earnings Quality
earningsQuality.title: Earnings Quality (OCF/Net Income)
earningsQuality.definition: Operating cash flow divided by net income. Measures whether reported profits are backed by real cash. A ratio above 1.0 means the company generates more cash than it reports as profit, indicating high quality earnings.
earningsQuality.example: A company with $5B cash flow and $4B net income has earnings quality of 1.25, where cash exceeds reported profits. If reversed ($3B cash, $5B profit), quality is 0.6, which is a red flag.

shareDilution.label: Share Dilution
shareDilution.title: Share Dilution / Buyback
shareDilution.definition: Year-over-year percentage change in shares outstanding. Negative values indicate share buybacks (fewer shares = more value per share). Positive values mean dilution (more shares = less value per share).
shareDilution.example: A -3% value means the company bought back 3% of its shares, so each remaining share now represents a larger slice of the business. +5% means 5% dilution.
